Luminary Conceptual Studio  ·  Baton Rouge, Louisiana

The Separation
Has Always Had a Name

St. George didn't begin in 2019. It began the day the suburbs decided the city was someone else's problem.

The Question

Can a community separate from a city's problems while depending on its proximity?

In October 2019, residents of a wealthy enclave in southeast Baton Rouge voted to incorporate as the city of St. George. It took five years of court battles to make it official.

The fight was described as being about schools, taxes, and local control. It was about all of those things. It was also about something older.

Shenandoah, Baton Rouge

86,000 People. One Decision.
Decades in the Making.

St. George is now Louisiana's fifth largest city. It did not appear from nowhere. It was assembled, street by street, subdivision by subdivision, across fifty years of deliberate choices about who belonged where.

The map was drawn long before anyone called it a city.

The Pattern

This story didn't start
in 2019.

1953 Baton Rouge hosts the first anti-segregation bus boycott in America. Two years before Montgomery. The city has been living with this tension longer than almost anywhere.
1970s Shenandoah is built. Marketing materials feature only white families. Streets named for Confederate generals — not accidentally. Intentionally.
2010 Residents push to break away from East Baton Rouge Parish schools. The school system is the mechanism. It always is.
2019 When the school district fails, the strategy shifts. If we can't have our schools, we'll have our city. The ballot measure passes, 54 percent.
2024 The Louisiana Supreme Court rules 4–3 in favor. St. George is official. Louisiana's newest city. Its fifth largest, at 86,000 people.

Shenandoah within East Baton Rouge Parish  ·  Louisiana

Shenandoah, Baton Rouge

The Suburb That
Started It All

Shenandoah was the largest residential development in Louisiana when it was built. Designed as a destination — for some. The community that launched the St. George movement grew up here, on streets named after battles fought to preserve a different kind of separation.

The names on the street signs were never accidental.

The Paradox

You cannot leave a city
and stay next to it at the same time.

St. George borders Baton Rouge on three sides. Its residents drive Baton Rouge roads, use Baton Rouge hospitals, cheer LSU in Baton Rouge, and fly out of Baton Rouge Metropolitan Airport.

The infrastructure, the culture, the economy — none of it stops at the new city line. The separation is legal. The dependence is real.

The Mississippi, Baton Rouge

The City Doesn't Know
It's Been Left.

Downtown Baton Rouge still hosts the concerts, the hospitals, the Louisiana State Capitol, LSU. The Mississippi still defines the western edge of everything. The things that make this region worth living near don't belong to St. George — and they never will.

Proximity is not the same as participation.

This Is Not Only About Baton Rouge

St. George is everywhere.
Baton Rouge just made it official.

From Atlanta to Memphis to Milwaukee, affluent communities have used school district boundaries, incorporation, and annexation resistance to achieve the same result St. George achieved through a ballot measure.

The mechanism changes. The pattern doesn't. What makes St. George different is that it did it openly, in public, and won.

What Becomes Possible

A City Still
Becoming Itself

St. George has something rare — a blank page. A city without a city hall, without an established identity, without a decision made yet about what it wants to be. That is either an opportunity or a missed one. The choice hasn't been made. Not yet.

The future rarely arrives. It reveals itself.

The Question

Can affluent suburbs politically separate from the problems of the larger city while continuing to benefit from being near it?

St. George suggests the answer is yes. What it hasn't answered — what no city like it ever has — is what that costs. Not in dollars. In something harder to measure.

"The city you leave
is still the city you need."
